actions.thought: Qc8d is a weak starting hand in a 3-handed game. Despite being on the button, playing Q8o out of position or in a multi-way pot is suboptimal. Folding is the most disciplined choice for the first hand to conserve chips while observing opponent play styles.

QTo is a strong enough hand to open-raise from the Small Blind after the Button folds. A 3x raise to 300 puts pressure on the Big Blind to fold marginal hands and gives me the initiative going into the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 8c Td. The Small Blind (Pro) raised to 300 after the Button folded. I need to call 200 more to see a flop, and I will have the advantage of being in position post-flop. Given the 2:1 pot odds and the wide range the Small Blind typically raises with, 8To is a standard defend.

On a dry, uncoordinated J-high flop, I have a significant range advantage as the pre-flop raiser, holding all the overpairs and strong top pairs. A small continuation bet of 1/3 pot (200) is highly effective at folding out the Big Blind's missed broadway cards, suited connectors, and sometimes even small pairs, allowing me to take down the pot cheaply with my missed QTo.

The flop of Jh-5c-4s does not connect with my 10-8o. The Small Blind (Pro) has led out with a continuation bet of 200 into a 600 pot. While the pot odds are 5:1, calling with 10-high and no real draws (only two weak overcards to the bottom pair) is generally unprofitable. I am out of position and have very little equity to continue in this hand against a range that includes Jacks, pocket pairs, or better high cards. Folding is the most prudent action.

7h 3c is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Even though I have position on the button, there is no strategic value in playing this hand unless I am attempting a pure steal, which is generally not optimal with complete trash. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

With the Button folding, I am in a heads-up situation against the Big Blind. King-Eight offsuit is a relatively strong hand for a Small Blind vs Big Blind battle. Raising here allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds immediately or play a pot with a hand that likely has better equity than a random Big Blind range. I will raise to 300.

I am in the big blind with 9c-Th, a very playable hand. The small blind has opened to 300. Given that I have already contributed 100 as the big blind, calling 200 is correct due to the favorable pot odds and my positional advantage over the small blind. Raising here is an option, but early in the tournament, a disciplined call to see a flop is the most effective way to realize my hand's equity.

On this low, connected flop of 2-3-5, I have missed completely with my King-high, but I maintain the initiative as the pre-flop aggressor. This board texture can be tricky, as Flashlight's defending range in the Big Blind often contains small pairs and suited connectors that connect here. However, many of his hands will also have missed. A continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) is appropriate to represent a strong overpair or to simply force folds from his weaker hands while keeping the pot manageable if I am called.

The flop of 2-3-5 is extremely unfavorable for my hand, 9-10. I have missed completely, and the board texture strongly favors the Small Blind's opening range, which likely contains many small pairs or high-card hands that currently beat my 9-high. With no pair, no draw, and no showdown value, calling a bet out of position is not mathematically sound. Folding is the most disciplined and effective way to preserve my stack for a better spot.

I am on the button with 9d 8c. In a 3-handed game, 98 offsuit is a reasonable hand to open with from the button to apply pressure on the blinds and take advantage of position. I will make a standard opening raise of 3x the big blind.

7h 8s is a weak offsuit connector that performs poorly out of position. Given that I am in the small blind and facing a raise from the button, my pot odds are not sufficient to justify a call, and 78o is not a strong enough candidate for a 3-bet bluff. Folding is the most disciplined and highest EV play to preserve my stack.
45 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor equity and terrible playability out of position. Facing a 3x raise from the button, folding is the standard and most profitable decision to preserve my stack.
59 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor equity. Even on the button, there is no strategic value in playing this hand against two opponents. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve stack depth.

With 64s in the Small Blind after the Button folds, I have a marginal but playable hand. Limping (calling 50) allows me to see a flop cheaply while keeping the pot small out of position. Raising is an option as a steal, but 64s lacks high card strength, making a limp or fold more standard. I'll opt to complete the blind.

I have J2o in the Big Blind. The Small Blind completed the bet, and the Button folded. J2o is a very weak hand, but since I can see the flop for free, checking is the most logical and standard play here.

On a Ks 4c 9c flop, I hit bottom pair with a marginal kicker. Betting into the Big Blind out of position with just bottom pair is risky, as I'll get called or raised by better hands (Kings, Nines, better 4s, or strong draws). Checking allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate based on Flash's action.
I have Jack-high with no draw on a King-high board. The Small Blind checked to me. There is no reason to bluff here as I have zero equity if called, and checking allows me to see a free turn card.

With bottom pair on a board containing multiple draws (clubs and spades), my hand has marginal showdown value but is far too weak to bet for value. Checking allows me to keep the pot small out of position, avoid getting raised off my equity, and evaluate Flash's action.

I have Jack-high with no showdown value and no draws on a King-high board. The Small Blind has checked to me on both the flop and the turn, indicating a weak range. However, my hand is extremely weak (J-high), and betting into a small pot without any equity if called is unnecessary. Checking allows me to see a free river card and potentially improve or reach showdown for free in a situation where I have very little invested.

On the river, my bottom pair of 4s has very little showdown value, especially with the 5 coming on the river and possible flush/straight draws completing. There is no reason to turn this into a bluff, and I certainly can't bet for value. Checking to reach a cheap showdown or fold to a bet is the safest and most standard line here.

I have Jack-high with no showdown value on a board where many hands beat me. Pro has checked the flop, turn, and river, which indicates a very weak range. However, since the pot is small (200 chips) and I have zero equity if called, bluffing is not worth the risk. Checking back allows me to reach showdown for free, even if I am unlikely to win.
I am dealt 93 offsuit on the button, which is an extremely weak hand. With two players left to act behind me, the correct move is to simply fold and wait for a better starting hand.

I am in the Small Blind with Q4o. The button (Pro) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Flashlight). Q4o is a weak hand, but since it is only 50 chips more to see a flop and the opponent has shown relatively tight-passive behavior so far, I will complete the bet to see a flop. If Flashlight raises, I can easily fold, but if they check, I get to see the board cheaply with a Queen-high hand.

I am in the big blind with Ace-Four offsuit after the small blind has completed the bet. Since I am out of position and have a decent starting hand that does not warrant a large raise, I will check to see the flop for free. This allows me to control the size of the pot and evaluate the flop texture before committing more chips.

I have bottom pair (fours) on an Ace-high board in a limped pot. This hand has some showdown value but is not strong enough to bet for value. I will check to control the pot size and see how Flashlight reacts.

I have flopped top and bottom two pair (Aces and Fours) on a relatively dry board. This is a very strong hand. Since the small blind checked to me, I should bet for value to build the pot and extract chips from weaker hands like an Ace with a smaller kicker, or middle/bottom pairs. A bet of 120 into a 200-chip pot represents a solid value bet that is likely to get called by hands I beat.

I have bottom pair (fours) with a Queen kicker. The pot was limped pre-flop, and Flashlight is betting 120 into a 200-chip pot. While an Ace is definitely in their range, they could also be betting an 8, a draw, or a total bluff to take down the pot. The price to call is reasonable (120 to win a total pot of 440), and folding bottom pair immediately in a limped pot might be too weak. I will call to see how the turn develops and evaluate their further aggression.

I have bottom pair (fours) on a board of As-8c-4d-2d. I called a bet on the flop to see if the turn would improve my hand or if the opponent would slow down. Since I am first to act on the turn, I will check to see how Flashlight responds. I have some showdown value, but my hand is not strong enough to bet for value or to turn into a bluff at this stage.

I have flopped top and bottom two pair, Aces and Fours, which is a very strong hand on this relatively dry board. Flash check-called my flop bet, indicating they likely hold an Ace with a weaker kicker or a middle pair. I want to continue extracting value while also charging any potential draws. A bet of 220 into the 440-chip pot is an optimal size to build the pot and get value from hands like A-X or pocket pairs.

I currently hold bottom pair (fours) on a board of As-8c-4d-2d. Flashlight has bet half-pot on both the flop and the turn after I limped from the Small Blind. This persistent aggression on an Ace-high board suggests they either have an Ace, a better pair (like an 8), or perhaps a strong draw. While the pot odds are 3:1, my hand is very weak and has little chance of improving to the best hand by the river. Continuing to call with bottom pair against two streets of betting is generally a losing play in the long run. I will fold now to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Holding K2o on the button in a 3-handed game is a marginal spot. While it's tempting to open-raise to steal the blinds, K2o is near the bottom of the range and doesn't play well post-flop if called. Given the stack depths and the fact that I've been active in previous hands, I will choose the conservative route and fold this weak king to maintain a solid image.

With QTo in the Small Blind against the Big Blind after the button folds, I have a strong hand heads-up. Raising allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or control the betting going into the flop. Since I am out of position post-flop, aggression is preferable to limping.

Facing a 3x open from the Small Blind, Pocket Eights is a strong hand that plays very well in position. While 3-betting is an option, calling keeps his weaker opening range intact, controls the size of the pot, and avoids a tricky situation if he 4-bets. With position post-flop, we can comfortably navigate most boards.

I have queen-high on a paired board (Js, Jd, 6c) and have essentially missed the flop completely. My hand has very little showdown value, and betting into a paired board is unlikely to generate enough folds against an opponent who defended the big blind, as they could easily be holding a jack or a pocket pair. Checking is the most disciplined and prudent play, allowing me to keep the pot small and re-evaluate my options on the turn or fold if the opponent bets.

The board is dry and paired. Flashlight checking likely indicates weakness, such as high cards that missed, but could occasionally be a trap. With pocket Eights, we have solid showdown value and don't need to bet for protection immediately. Checking behind exercises pot control, keeps his range wide, and allows us to evaluate the turn in position.

The board is paired with two Jacks, and I hold Queen-high with no pair or drawing potential. Both the opponent and I checked the flop, indicating a lack of strong connections for either player. Since I am out of position and have no equity, betting into the opponent is unlikely to succeed as a bluff and only serves to inflate the pot when I am behind. Checking is the most prudent strategy to keep the pot controlled and potentially see a free river card, or fold if the opponent decides to make a significant bet.

Flashlight has checked twice, which usually indicates weakness, such as Ace-high, King-high, or a smaller pocket pair. With pocket Eights, we have a strong two pair (Jacks and Eights) and are likely ahead. Betting here gets value from draws that picked up on the turn, smaller pairs, and stubborn Ace-highs, while also denying equity to overcards. A half-pot bet of 300 is an optimal size to extract value and protect our hand.

The board is paired (Js-Jd-6c-3s), and I hold Queen-high with no pair, no draw, and essentially zero equity. Checking to the opponent on the turn and facing a bet of 300 into a 600 pot indicates the opponent either has a made hand (a Jack, a pair of 6s, or a pocket pair) or is attempting to take down the pot with a probe bet. Since I am out of position with no showdown value, calling is non-profitable as I am effectively bluff-catching with the worst possible hand against a standard betting range. Folding is the most disciplined and conservative play to preserve my stack.

I am in the button position with 8h 2h, which is an extremely weak hand with very little equity or playability. Even in a 3-handed game, playing this hand from the button is a negative expected value (-EV) move. I will fold to conserve my stack and wait for a more advantageous opportunity to enter a pot.

Pocket Jacks is a premium starting hand. Being first to act in the small blind against the big blind (after the button folded), I will raise to 300 to build the pot, extract value, and avoid playing a multiway pot out of position or letting the BB see a free flop with random overcards.

I am in the Big Blind with 6h 7c. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. In a three-handed game, I have position on the Small Blind post-flop. I need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 600, giving me 3:1 pot odds. While 67o is a marginal hand, the combination of pot odds and being in position makes this a standard call to see a flop in a short-handed tournament setting.

Holding an overpair (Jacks) on a wet and draw-heavy flop (7d 9c 5d), it is crucial to bet for value and protection. There are numerous flush draws, open-ended straight draws, and top pair hands (like a 9) in the Big Blind's range that will call. Betting half the pot charges these draws to see the turn and maintains my initiative in the hand.

I have hit middle pair on the flop with an open-ended straight draw (6, 7 on a 7-9-5 board). This is a very strong hand with significant equity against the Small Blind's range. The opponent's bet is 1/2 pot, which is standard. Given the flush draw on the board and the fact that I have both a pair and a monster draw, calling is a solid play to control the pot and keep the opponent's bluffs in while maintaining position for the turn.

The Ace of Diamonds is a terrible turn card for my pocket Jacks, as it completes the obvious diamond flush draw and hits any Ace-high floats that may have called my flop bet. Although I hold the Jack of Diamonds which gives me a redraw to a high flush, betting here would likely only be called or raised by better hands such as flushes, two pairs, or an Ace. Checking allows me to control the size of the pot, keep Flash's bluffs in his range, and evaluate his next move without unnecessarily risking more chips.

The Ace of Diamonds on the turn is a significant scare card. While I have middle pair and an open-ended straight draw, the Ace likely hits the Small Blind's pre-flop raising range. By checking back, I control the pot size and take a free card to try and hit my straight on the river, while also maintaining some showdown value with my pair of sevens. Pro's check could be a trap or a sign of weakness, but checking behind is the safest way to realize my equity in this spot.

The 8 of Clubs on the river doesn't improve my pocket Jacks and potentially completes some straight draws like 6x. With the Ace of Diamonds still on the board and potential flushes out there, my hand primarily functions as a bluff-catcher with showdown value. Betting here would likely fold out worse hands (like a pair of nines or tens) while getting called or raised by any Ace, flush, or straight. Checking allows me to reach showdown cheaply or induce a bluff from a missed draw.

The river 8c has completed my straight (5-6-7-8-9). Pro, after raising pre-flop and betting the flop, has checked both the turn (when the third diamond appeared) and the river. While a flush is possible given the three diamonds on the board, Pro's passive play on the turn and river makes a flush or a slow-played set less likely. I have a very strong hand that should be bet for value. I am targeting an Ace or perhaps a lower pair that Pro is unwilling to bet himself but might call with. A bet of 800 into a 1200 pot represents a strong value-oriented sizing.

The board is extremely dangerous, featuring a completed flush draw, an overcard Ace, and straight possibilities (such as 6x, T8, or 86). My pocket Jacks have essentially been reduced to a weak bluff-catcher. Flash's sizable river bet of 800 into a 1200 pot polarizes his range, heavily representing a straight, a flush, or an Ace that improved. While he could be bluffing with missed non-diamond draws, my hand is too weak to call down such a large bet on a board that hits his defending range very well. Folding is the safest and most optimal play here.

J8s is a playable suited one-gapper. On the Button in a 3-handed game, it falls well within a standard open-raising range. I'm raising to 3 BB to apply pressure on the blinds, take the initiative, and utilize my positional advantage post-flop.

I hold Ac 9c in the Small Blind. This is a very strong hand in a three-handed game, especially against a standard button open from Pro. While calling is an option, 3-betting to 900 is more effective to take the initiative, potentially win the pot immediately, and prevent the Big Blind from realizing their equity cheaply. It also forces Pro to play a larger pot out of position if they choose to call.

KJs is a premium broadway hand, making it very strong in a 3-handed game. Facing a button raise and a small blind 3-bet, I am getting good pot odds to call. Folding would be too conservative given my stack depth, and 4-betting creates unnecessary variance at this stage. Calling allows me to see a flop with a high-equity hand in position relative to the original raiser, while still playing carefully against the 3-better.

We opened J8 suited on the Button, which is a standard steal attempt. However, facing a 3-bet from the Small Blind and a cold-call from the Big Blind, our hand is severely outclassed. Both opponents are indicating very strong ranges, and J8s will frequently be dominated or drawing dead. Folding is the easiest and most profitable decision to preserve our stack.

I have flopped Top Pair Top Kicker on a dry board of 2s-4h-9d. After my pre-flop 3-bet was called by the Big Blind, I am in a strong position. I will lead with a continuation bet of 700, which is approximately one-third of the pot. This sizing allows me to extract value from middle pairs like 55-88 and high-card hands that might feel priced in to call one street, while also protecting my equity against overcards.

The opponent, Flash, has continuation-bet a small amount (700 into 2800, which is 25%) on a dry board (9-4-2). With King-Jack suited, I hold two overcards, giving me reasonable equity against a range that includes small pairs, missed overcards, or total bluffs. The pot odds (1:4) make it a mathematically sound call to see a turn card, as folding would be too passive given the small sizing, and raising would unnecessarily bloat the pot with only high-card strength. I will call the 700 and re-evaluate on the turn.

The Queen on the turn is an overcard to my pair of nines and potentially hits the calling range of Flashlight (hands like AQ, KQ, or QJ). Since I am out of position and the board has become more coordinated for the opponent's range, checking is the most prudent play. It allows me to control the size of the pot and evaluate Flashlight's action. If they check back, I likely still have the best hand; if they bet, I can decide whether to call based on the sizing and the board texture.

Flash checked the turn after a small flop c-bet. My King-high has very little showdown value, and betting would likely only get called by better hands while forcing out bluffs that I currently beat. Checking allows me to see the river for free and preserves my stack.

On this river, my pair of nines remains a solid showdown hand but is unlikely to get value from worse hands if I bet. Flashlight's check on the turn after calling the flop suggests they have a marginal hand like a middle pair (55-88) or perhaps a weak nine. The Queen is a scare card that hit their potential float range (AQ, KQ, QJ), and the river 4 is mostly a brick unless they specifically have A4s or 54s. By checking, I am looking to realize my showdown value against their medium pairs. If Flashlight bets, I will have to decide based on sizing, but checking is the best way to avoid losing more chips to a Queen or trips while still winning the pot against their missed hands or smaller pairs.

The board is paired on the river (4d), and I hold King-Jack high. Flash bet the flop into two players but checked both the turn and the river. My hand has very little showdown value, effectively losing to any pair (a 9, a Queen, or even a pocket pair like 2s). Betting here would be a pure bluff against a range that likely consists of showdown-worthy hands (like a 9 or pocket pairs) that are unlikely to fold. Given Flash showed aggression on the flop, there is a reasonable chance they have a piece of this board. Checking behind allows me to go to showdown without losing more chips on a likely losing hand, which is the most prudent strategy.

I am currently on the button with a suited Jack and the chip lead. In a 3-handed game, opening wide from the button is a standard aggressive strategy to put pressure on the blinds and utilize positional advantage. J4 suited is strong enough to open for a raise, and a 3x sizing (300 chips) has been the consistent open size in this game so far.

8-J offsuit is a weak starting hand to play out of position from the small blind. Given that Flash is the button and has been playing aggressively, defending this hand would lead to a difficult post-flop scenario with poor reverse implied odds. Folding is the most disciplined and long-term profitable decision to preserve my stack for better spots.

K7 offsuit is a weak holding, especially when facing a 3bb open raise from the button. Playing this out of position will lead to tough spots on many post-flop textures, as we will often be dominated when hitting top pair. Folding and preserving the stack for better opportunities is the most solid play.

In a 3-handed game, position is paramount. As the button, I have the advantage and the opportunity to control the pot. While 4hTh is a speculative suited hand, it is strong enough to play in this position. I will open-raise to 250, which is 2.5x the big blind, to apply pressure to the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop or see a flop with the betting lead.

I have pocket Aces, the absolute best starting hand, in the small blind. Facing a button raise to 250, this is a mandatory 3-bet for value. Sizing up to 900 out of position is a standard roughly 3.5x multiplier that will build the pot while charging the button or big blind to see a flop.

I am in the Big Blind with a small pocket pair (4d 4s). Flashlight opened from the Button to 250, and Pro 3-bet from the Small Blind to 900. To call, I need to commit 800 more chips. My effective stack against Pro is 8700. For set-mining with a small pair, I generally look for implied odds of at least 15:1. Here, the ratio is approximately 10.8:1 (8700 / 800). Additionally, I am out of position against both players and Flashlight still has the option to 4-bet or call behind me. Given the high 3-bet sizing and the unfavorable implied odds, folding is the most disciplined play to protect my stack.

4hTh is a suited hand, but it is still a weak holding to continue with after Pro's 3-bet to 900. Investing 650 more chips into the pot against a significant show of strength from the Small Blind is a negative expectation play. It is wiser to preserve my stack and wait for a better spot to utilize my position.

J5o is a very weak, unsuited, and disconnected hand. Even being on the button, it is not worth an open-raise or a limp, as it plays extremely poorly post-flop and has negative expected value against the blinds' defending ranges. The optimal play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

In a blind vs. blind situation (Pro folded), Queen-Five suited is a strong enough hand to exert pressure. I am the chip leader, and Flashlight (BB) has shown some willingness to fold to pre-flop aggression in previous hands. Raising to 300 total (a 3x raise) is a standard play to take down the blinds or play the pot with initiative and decent post-flop equity.

My hand (2d, 4d) is very weak, and I am out of position against an opponent who raised from the small blind. The risk of calling 200 chips for such a marginal hand does not justify the potential reward, especially given that I have little equity against a raising range. Folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound play here.

I am on the button with King-Deuce offsuit (K2o). While K-high has some value in a three-handed game, K2o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop and doesn't stand up well to a three-bet. With Pro in the big blind having shown a willingness to play aggressively in previous hands, I will opt for a fold here to maintain my chip lead and wait for a more favorable hand or a better spot to exert pressure.

I am in the small blind with Q7o after the button folds. Completing the small blind is a standard play here to see a flop with a decent, albeit marginal, holding, given that I am already committed to 50 chips and only need to call 50 more to close the action pre-flop.

I have 6c4c in the Big Blind facing a Small Blind limp. This is a speculative suited gapper. Raising could fold out some better hands but might bloat the pot unnecessarily with a marginal holding, whereas checking allows me to see a free flop with position on the SB, where I can realize equity easily and outplay them post-flop.

I have top pair (Q-7) on a relatively dry board (J-Q-6). While my kicker is weak, this is likely the best hand currently given that the Big Blind checked. Betting allows me to extract value from smaller pairs or draws and better define the opponent's range. I will bet 130 into the 200-chip pot to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable.

We flopped bottom pair with a weak kicker and a backdoor flush draw on a J-Q-6 board. Facing a bet of 130 into a pot of 200, our equity against a range of queens, jacks, and straight draws is quite poor. The implied odds aren't good enough to justify continuing, and we will frequently face tough spots on the turn. Folding is the safest and most optimal play here.

QhJs is a strong starting hand for a 3-handed game, particularly from the button. Given my position, I have the advantage and should look to take the initiative pre-flop. Raising to 300 is a standard sizing to build the pot and potentially win the blinds or dictate the action post-flop.

My hand is K3 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. I am in the small blind facing a 3x raise from the Button. Playing K3o out of position against a pre-flop raise is mathematically unprofitable. The best action is to simply fold and preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with 6c 7d. Flashlight (Button) raised to 300, which is a 3x open. Although I am getting roughly 2.25:1 odds to call (200 to win a pot of 450), 76o is a weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive button raise. In a three-handed game, I should defend the BB reasonably wide, but 76o is at the bottom of the range and playing it out of position often leads to difficult spots on the flop. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

K7o is a relatively weak and easily dominated hand, even when playing on the Button. In a 3-handed game, raising as a steal might be considered, but with both the Small Blind and Big Blind left to act, and Flash being active in previous hands, it's safer to fold this unsuited, marginal holding and wait for a better spot.

Pro folded on the button, leaving the action to me in the Small Blind. I have 2h3h, which is a very weak hand but suited. Given that I only need to commit 50 more chips to see a flop in a pot that will be 200 chips, I am getting 3:1 pot odds. I will call to see if I can hit a strong draw or a lucky flop while keeping the pot small.

I am in the big blind with Ace-Nine offsuit after the small blind completed their bet. This is a strong enough hand to see a flop cheaply. Checking allows me to realize my equity and keep the pot manageable while playing in position against the small blind.

I have 2h3h on a TsQs5h board, giving me no pair and only a very backdoor heart flush draw. I am out of position against Flashlight in the Big Blind. There is no reason to lead out with a bet here, as the board favors many hands in the Big Blind's range. I will check and likely fold to any significant bet, hoping for a free turn card.

The flop is Ts-Qs-5h and I hold Ac-9h. I have overcards but no made hand or draw, so there is very little value in betting here. Checking behind allows me to realize my equity for free and prevents me from bloathing the pot when I am likely behind. This is a standard play to keep the pot controlled.

The turn is the 8 of spades, making the board Ts Qs 5h 8s. I have 2h 3h, which has no value and no real draws. I am out of position. I checked the flop and Flashlight checked behind. I will check again on the turn. I have no reason to bluff into this board given the lack of equity.

The board has three spades, and holding Ace-high with no spade, I have very little equity to continue if the opponent has anything. Betting would be a pure bluff that is unlikely to force a fold given the board texture. Checking behind allows me to reach the river for free and minimize losses.

I have 2h3h, which has zero showdown value on this TsQs5h8sQc board. The pot is small (200 chips), and both Flashlight and I have checked the flop and turn. While I could attempt a bluff to represent a Queen or a missed draw, the risk-to-reward ratio in a 200-chip pot isn't favorable. Flashlight's passive play could be a weak pair (5s or 8s) or even a Ten looking for a cheap showdown. I will check and concede the pot, as even Ace-high beats me.

With the board pairing the Queen on the river, my Ace-high has become a pair of Queens with an Ace kicker. Flash checked the river, indicating a likely weak or marginal hand. There is little value in betting here, as I am unlikely to be called by a worse hand, and checking allows me to go to showdown to potentially win against a missed draw or lower kicker.

I am on the button with 75o. While this is a weak hand, previous hands (3, 9, 11) have shown that Flashlight and Pro are currently over-folding their blinds to button raises. In a 3-handed game, maintaining aggression from the button is key. Since I have successfully stolen the blinds in all three of my recent button opportunities, I will continue to apply pressure with a standard 3x open raise, expecting a high fold equity.

My hand, 4s 7d, is extremely weak and unplayable, especially from the small blind out of position against an open-raise from the button. There is no strategic value in defending this hand. Folding is the standard, high-level play to conserve chips and wait for a stronger starting hand.

I have 9d 6c offsuit, which is a very weak, disconnected hand. Facing a 3x raise from the Button, calling out of position is extremely unprofitable because the hand plays terribly post-flop and rarely makes strong hands. Folding is the straightforward and correct play to preserve my stack.

I am in the small blind holding pocket threes on a board of 4-2-6-2-8. The river card is an 8, which doesn't significantly change the board texture. Flashlight has checked for the third consecutive time after calling my flop continuation bet. I have top pair with an Ace, which is currently the best hand on this relatively dry board. 79o is a weak, unsuited gapper. Even on the button, it is generally not profitable to open or call with this hand. I will fold and wait for a better starting hand.

8-10 offsuit is a weak hand to defend with, especially playing out of position in the small blind against an active button raiser. Calling would put me in a disadvantaged position for the remainder of the hand with marginal cards. It is better to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.

KJs is a premium starting hand in a 3-handed game, especially when sitting on the button. I want to raise to build the pot and take control of the hand. A 3x raise to 300 has been my standard sizing and is effective here.

I am holding an 8-J on a T-K-9-2 board, which gives me a double gutshot straight draw (requiring a 7 or a Q to complete). I am holding Jack-high on a board of T-K-9-2-5. My hand has no showdown value and missed all draws. The board shows Ac Kd Ad, and I hold 9d 3d, giving me a flush draw. With the pot at 600 and a bet of 200, I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call. The turn card is the ACE OF HEARTS (Ah), making the board ACE OF CLUBS (Ac), KING OF DIAMONDS (Kd), ACE OF DIAMONDS (Ad), ACE OF HEARTS (Ah). I have three of a kind (Aces) with a King-Jack kicker. The board shows three Aces and a King. This creates a very static situation where the best five-card hand is determined by the board and the highest kicker unless someone holds the case Ace. The board currently shows three Aces (Ac, Ad, Ah) and a King (Kd), with a 5s on the river. My best five-card hand is Three of a Kind (Aces) with a King-Jack kicker (A-A-A-K-J). The board shows four Aces (Ac, Ad, Ah and the Ac from the flop) and a King. I am on the button with 2d 5h, which is an extremely weak hand. While I have the chip lead and positional advantage, this hand has very little potential and is generally considered trash. I am in the small blind with a very weak hand (5-2 offsuit). Playing out of position with such a poor hand is strategically unsound, even with the cheap price to complete the blind. It is better to conserve my chips and fold, waiting for a more advantageous situation.

I have top pair on a monotone board in a 3-bet pot. While I don't hold a diamond in my hand, top pair with a Ten kicker is currently the likely best hand. Flash checked to me on the turn after leading the flop. With the Jack of Diamonds giving me a flush draw on a board of 2d-8d-Ad-8c, checking behind is the most prudent strategy. I have two pair, Aces and Eights, with a Ten kicker on a board that contains three diamonds and a pair of eights. Pre-flop, I 3-bet and led on the flop, which Flashlight called. I have Queen-high with no pair on a board of A-8-2-8-7. I have no showdown value against any pair. However, with only 4 direct outs and no backdoor flush draw, my equity is significantly lower than the pot odds required to call. With suited broadway cards (JsQs) in the button position, this is a strong opening hand. A standard raise to 300 is the correct play to exert pressure on the blinds and potentially secure the pot or play a hand in position.

I am in the Small Blind facing a raise from the Button. My hand, Queen-Four offsuit, is a very weak and disconnected hand with low equity. Playing it out of position against a raiser is unprofitable, so the standard decision is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 7h 3d, which is a very weak hand. Flashlight raised from the button to 300. Although I am getting roughly 2.25-to-1 odds to call, this hand plays extremely poorly post-flop and I would be out of position for the rest of the hand. Given my healthy stack, there is no reason to defend with trash hands.

I am on the Button with Ace-Ten offsuit. In a 3-handed game, this is a strong starting hand and a clear open-raise. A standard raise size of 3 big blinds (300) puts pressure on the blinds and gives me the initiative going into the flop with position.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Seven offsuit. The Button (Pro) has opened for 300. T7o is a weak hand to play out of position against a button raise, especially with the Big Blind still to act. Folding is the standard and most prudent play here to preserve my stack.

I hold a pocket pair (3c, 3s) in the Big Blind. Given that the Small Blind has folded and the Button has raised to 300, I have favorable pot odds to call and see a flop, hoping to hit a set. Raising with pocket threes out of position is generally too aggressive, so calling is the most sound strategic play.

I am in the Big Blind and first to act on a very wet, connected board (7d, 6d, 4d). I hold pocket threes with no diamond, meaning I have limited equity and no flush draw. Given that I am out of position against the aggressor, leading out would be weak and likely result in me folding to a raise. Checking is the standard play to gauge the opponent's reaction to this board texture.

I have the nut flush draw on a monotone flop with the Ace of Diamonds. Flashlight checked to me, giving me the opportunity to take control of the hand. A continuation bet here serves as a strong semi-bluff, applying pressure on weaker holdings or lower flush draws, while building the pot for when I hit my flush. Betting around half-pot (300) is a standard and effective sizing.

The board texture is extremely wet (7d, 6d, 4d), offering flush and straight possibilities. Holding pocket threes with no diamond, I have almost zero equity against a betting range on this board. Even if the opponent is bluffing with high cards, I have no redraws and will be forced to fold on almost any turn card. Continuing here is throwing chips away; folding is the disciplined, correct play.

I am on the button with J2s. In a three-handed game, this hand is marginal but playable for an open raise to steal the blinds. However, looking at the previous hands, the blinds (Flashlight and Pro) have been quite active in defending and 3-betting. Specifically, Flashlight has 3-bet me multiple times recently (Hand #71, Hand #57, Hand #47). With a weak suited hand like J2h, I risk getting into a difficult spot if I raise and face a 3-bet, or if I get called and miss the flop out of position. Given the stack sizes and the aggression levels shown by the opponents, I will choose to fold this hand and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

I am in the Small Blind and the Button (Flash) has folded, leaving me heads-up against the Big Blind (Pro). Given that I only need to call 50 more to see a flop, and 7hJs is a reasonably playable hand in this position, I will call to see the flop and potentially outmaneuver the Big Blind.
With 2c 3s in the Big Blind facing a limp from the Small Blind, I have the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. I will gladly check my option to see a free flop.

The board (5c 4h 9c) is not particularly favorable for my hand (7h Js). I only have a weak gutshot draw (needing a 6 to complete a 4-5-6-7 straight). Since the Big Blind checked, the most prudent strategic move is to check behind to control the pot size and take a free card. There is no reason to bluff here as the board does not offer significant equity to represent.

With 2-3 on a 5-4-9 flop, I have flopped an open-ended straight draw. Since the pot was limped pre-flop and Flashlight checked to me, I have a great opportunity to semi-bluff. I have no showdown value currently, but my draw has significant equity. Betting here puts pressure on my opponent's weak holdings, potentially winning the pot right now, while building it in case I hit my straight on the turn.

The opponent has bet 150 into a 250 pot on a board of 5c 4h 9c. My hand (7h Js) is only Jack-high with no significant draws. The board is wet and provides various straight and pair possibilities that hit a standard big blind range well. Without a pair or a strong draw, continuing against a bet is mathematically unfavorable and risky. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for a better spot.

KhJs is a strong hand, especially in position on the button. Opening with a raise is standard to take the initiative, put pressure on the blinds, and potentially win the pot pre-flop or control the hand post-flop.
K7o is too weak of a hand to play from the small blind against a button raise, especially with the big blind still left to act. The best play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am holding Ace-Six of Diamonds in the Big Blind. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the Button (standard 3x). Flashlight is the shortest stack at the table with about 60 big blinds. A6s is a very strong hand three-handed and definitely too strong to fold. While I could 3-bet to put pressure on the Button, calling allows me to play a pot in position relative to the initial raiser (since I'm in the BB) and keeps their range wider. Given my stack size and the current tournament stage, I want to see a flop. I will call the additional 200.

I have the nut flush draw with Ace-Six of Diamonds on a 5d-2d-9h board. I am out of position against Flashlight, who was the pre-flop aggressor. In this situation, the standard play is to check to the raiser to see how they react. If they bet, I have a very strong draw and can evaluate whether to call or check-raise based on their bet sizing. Checking allows me to control the pot size or potentially trap if I hit my flush on the turn.

I am the aggressor on a somewhat dry board with two diamonds. I hold two overcards (King and Jack) and a backdoor flush draw. My opponent (the big blind) checked to me, so I should maintain the initiative by continuation betting. A small bet of 300 into the 650 pot should be sufficient to represent strength and potentially take down the pot if my opponent has missed, or to build the pot if I improve on the turn.

I am holding the nut flush draw with Ace-Six of Diamonds on a Five-Deuce-Nine board with two diamonds. Flashlight has made a standard continuation bet of 300 into a 650 pot. This is an easy call given my equity (roughly 35% to hit the flush by the river). Calling allows me to keep Flashlight's bluffs in and see a turn card relatively cheaply without inflating the pot while out of position. If I hit the diamond on the turn, I will be in a strong position to extract value from a shorter-stacked opponent.

I have completed the nut flush with the Ace of Diamonds. Flashlight was the pre-flop and flop aggressor. In this situation, I will check to them again. This serves two purposes: it allows them to continue bluffing if they have nothing, and it disguises the strength of my hand. If I lead out (donk bet) now that the flush has completed, it is very obvious what I have. By checking, I give Flashlight the opportunity to bet their overpairs, sets, or smaller flushes, at which point I can either call to trap or check-raise to build the pot against their shorter stack.

I have King-Jack offsuit on a board of 5d 2d 9h 3d. I have no flush draw and only a gutshot straight draw (needing a 4). My opponent called my flop bet, which suggests they have some part of the board, potentially a pair or a draw of their own. Betting now is risky as it builds a pot with a weak hand and might get blown off my equity by a check-raise. Checking behind allows me to see the river for free, preserving my stack and keeping the pot controlled. If I miss on the river, I can safely fold to a bet.

I have the nut flush with the Ace of Diamonds on a board that has now paired on the river (5d-2d-9h-3d-9s). Although the board pairing introduces the slight possibility of a full house, the nut flush is still an incredibly strong hand in a three-handed game. After I called the flop and Flashlight checked the turn, it's likely they have a marginal made hand like a 9, an overpair, or a smaller flush that they might be willing to pay off a value bet with. If I check, I risk them checking back for a free showdown. Therefore, I will lead out with a value bet of approximately 65% of the pot to extract chips from their remaining stack.

The board shows 5d 2d 9h 3d 9s. My hand, KhJs, has no pairs and no flush. With a bet from Flash (the big blind) on the river, I am effectively holding air. There is no reason to call, as I cannot beat any value range or even most bluffs. Folding is the only logical move as I have zero equity to win at showdown.

In 3-handed play, being on the Button gives me positional advantage, but 85s is too weak and disconnected to justify an opening raise. There is no need to bloat the pot with a marginal hand when there will be better spots to steal or play for value.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Ten offsuit. The button has folded, leaving only the Big Blind (Flashlight) to beat. JTo is a strong starting hand in a heads-up blind-versus-blind scenario. I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand and build the pot or take the blinds immediately.

I am in the Big Blind and facing a raise to 300 from the Small Blind. I have suited connectors (7c-Jc), which are playable in this position. Given the pot odds of 2:1 (calling 200 to win a pot of 400), this is a standard defensive call. I will take the flop and see how the board texture develops.

I am the pre-flop aggressor in a blind-versus-blind pot. The flop of 4-2-3 is low and favors the caller's range slightly more than mine, but as the initial raiser, I should still represent strength. I have two overcards (Jack and Ten) which gives me some equity if called. I will place a continuation bet of 350 (slightly over half-pot) to try and take the pot down now or to force the opponent to define their hand.

The flop texture (4-2-3) is highly coordinated and favorable for my calling range as the Big Blind. While my hand (Jack-high) is currently behind, I possess a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5) and two overcards. The pot odds of approximately 27% (calling 350 to a total pot of 1300) justify a call to see a turn card, as my opponent is likely making a standard continuation bet on this board texture.

On the turn, the 9d doesn't significantly change the board texture. I have Jack-high with no draws. Having been called on the flop, my opponent likely has a piece of the board or a small pair. Since I have no showdown value and no equity to improve to a winning hand, I will check to see how the opponent reacts. If they check back, I might have a chance to see a cheap river or evaluate a bluffing opportunity then; if they bet, I will likely fold as I have nothing to continue with.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-high, facing a check from Flash on the turn. The board is relatively dry (4-2-3-9), and my hand has minimal showdown value. Betting here would be a pure bluff, which is risky given the opponent's previous aggression. Checking behind allows me to realize my equity for free and potentially see a river card without committing more chips to the pot, maintaining control.

On the river, the board has paired the three and completed a backdoor diamond flush. I have Jack-high with no showdown value. Throughout the tournament, I have observed that Flashlight often folds to river aggression when they haven't shown strength on previous streets (as seen in Hand #82). My check on the turn might look like a trapped monster or a missed draw, but by betting the river, I can represent a three, a nine, or the backdoor flush. A bet of 750 into a 1300 pot (roughly 57% pot) is a sizing that applies significant pressure to Ace-high hands and weak pairs that Flashlight might be holding.

The board texture is 4d-2s-3h-9d-3d. I hold Jack-high (7c-Jc), which has essentially no showdown value. My opponent, Flash, has bet 750 into a 1300 pot on the river. This bet sizing on a board paired with threes likely indicates that my opponent either has a made hand—such as a pocket pair, a three, or even a straight with 5-6—or is betting their equity aggressively. Given my lack of any pair and the high probability that I am beaten, calling would be a losing play. Therefore, folding is the only logical strategic decision.

I am on the button with Ace-Deuce suited (Ac 2c). In a three-handed game, any suited Ace is a very strong hand, especially from late position. Raising is the standard play here to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately. Throughout the tournament, the standard opening raise has been 300, so I will stick with that sizing to maintain consistency and put pressure on Flashlight and Pro.

I am in the small blind with a pocket pair of fours. While pocket fours are vulnerable, they have good set-mining potential. Given the stack depths and the fact that the button (Flash) has been playing aggressively, calling to see a flop is the most balanced approach. I am looking to hit a set of fours or play conservatively on the post-flop if the board is overcards.

Facing a button raise and a small blind call, A2s is an excellent hand to defend in the big blind. We are getting fantastic pot odds (200 to call into a pot of 700) and A2s plays very well multi-way, as it can flop nut flush draws and wheel straight draws. While squeezing is a viable option, calling is the most standard and lowest-variance profitable play here, particularly given the small blind's middling stack.

I am in the small blind with a pair of fours on a board of 10-10-5. My hand is currently a weak bluff-catcher. Since I am first to act on this flop, checking is the standard play. I want to control the size of the pot and see if my opponents check behind or show strength before committing more chips. With the board paired and no improvement for my pocket fours, betting would likely only be called by better hands or fold out bluffs I might want to keep in the hand.

I missed the flop completely with Ace-high and no draws on a paired, two-flush board. With two opponents remaining, including the pre-flop raiser yet to act behind me, the best and most standard play is to check and prepare to fold to any aggression.

On a flop of Tc-Td-5c, I have the nut flush draw with Ac 2c. After both opponents checked to me, I want to continue my aggression as the pre-flop raiser. A continuation bet here allows me to take down the pot immediately if they missed, or build a larger pot for if I hit my flush. Given the paired board, a bet of roughly half the pot is a standard sizing to maintain pressure while balanced with my range.

The board is paired with two Tens, and I only have a pocket pair of fours. This is a very dry, unfavorable board for my hand as it hits the opening range of the Button (Flash) significantly harder than mine. Flash has bet 450 into a pot of 900, which is a standard continuation bet or value bet. I am at the bottom of my range and have almost no equity; my only way to win is to improve to a set of fours, which are two outs. Calling a bet here is mathematically incorrect and puts me in a difficult spot for future streets out of position. Folding is the clear, disciplined play.

We completely missed the flop, holding only Ace-high with a backdoor spade draw on a paired board that also features a flush draw. Facing a half-pot continuation bet from the pre-flop raiser, continuing out of position with such a weak hand would be highly unprofitable. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am in the button position with a strong starting hand (Ace-King offsuit). Given my stack size and the strength of the cards, I should raise to build the pot and potentially isolate one of the blinds to gain initiative in the hand.
4h2s is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the Button, folding from the Small Blind is the only reasonable play.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Ten offsuit. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the Button. Given the pot odds (200 to call into a pot of 450) and the strength of QTo relative to a Button opening range, this is a standard defend. I have a significant chip lead over Flashlight, and while I will be out of position post-flop, QTo is too strong to fold here. I will call to see a flop.

I have missed the flop entirely with Queen-Ten offsuit on a King-high board. As the defender in the Big Blind, the standard procedure is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. I have no pair and no real draw, so I will check and see how the button responds.

I have top pair with a top kicker (Ace-King) on a relatively dry, rainbow board. Since the opponent checked, I should bet for value to get chips from weaker Kings or middle pairs like 5-x, while also maintaining the initiative I established pre-flop. A bet of 350 into the 650 pot is a standard continuation bet to protect my hand and extract value.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Ten offsuit. After defending pre-flop, the flop comes King-high (Kh-5d-2h). I checked to the pre-flop aggressor, Flashlight, who has now made a continuation bet of 350 into a 650 pot. My hand has no pair, no draws, and very little backdoor equity. While I have a significant chip lead, there is no reason to float or bluff on this board texture out of position with zero equity. The most disciplined and standard play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am first to act on the Button with A♦ 4♦. This is a strong suited Ace that plays very well in position, both as a steal and post-flop. A standard open to 3x (300) will pressure the blinds and often take down the pot right here, while still leaving me with good playability if called.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Deuce of clubs. While the hand is suited, it is very weak (J2s). The Button (Pro) has opened for 300. Defending this hand out of position against a 3x raise is generally not profitable, especially since I'll be playing the rest of the hand out of position against the chip leader's primary challenger. Given my healthy stack, there is no need to make a marginal defense here.

Pocket nines are a strong holding against a button open, especially in a heads-up scenario. By 3-betting to 900, I can take the initiative, potentially thin the field or win the pot pre-flop, and extract value from worse hands that may have opened on the button.

Facing a 3-bet from the Big Blind, my A4s is a marginal hand. While it has some playability and I have position, calling a 3-bet often leads to reverse implied odds if an Ace flops, as I'll likely be outkicked by their value range. Given Flashlight's relatively short stack (about 50bb), 4-bet bluffing is risky, and flatting is generally -EV compared to simply letting it go. Folding is the most prudent action here to preserve my stack for better spots.

I am on the button with 9h 5d, which is a very weak starting hand. Although I am the chip leader and have been aggressive from the button in previous hands, this specific hand does not warrant a raise or a call against the blinds, especially since Pro and Flashlight have shown willingness to defend or 3-bet. I will wait for a better spot to utilize my stack advantage.

I am in the small blind with a strong pocket pair, Tens. The button has folded, leaving me heads-up against the big blind. Raising here is the standard, aggressive play to isolate the opponent, build the pot, and capitalize on my hand strength.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Three offsuit, which is a very weak and disconnected hand. The Small Blind has raised to 3x the big blind. Even though I would have position post-flop, J3o lacks the equity and playability needed to profitably defend against a raise. Folding is the best decision to preserve my stack.

I am in the Button position with Ace-Jack suited (Ad, Jd), which is a strong starting hand. Given my stack size and position, raising is the optimal move to take initiative and build the pot. A raise to 300 is consistent with the standard bet sizing observed in previous hands.

T9o is too weak to play from the Small Blind against a Button open. We will be out of position post-flop and face the risk of a squeeze from the Big Blind who has a large stack. Folding is the standard and most profitable decision here.

I am in the Big Blind with 7h5h, which is a suited semi-connector. Flashlight (Button) has raised to 300. It costs me 200 more to call, and I am getting roughly 2.25:1 odds on a call. Given the playability of suited connectors and the fact that I am the chip leader, I will call to see a flop. I am out of position, but the price is right to defend my blind against a Button open.

On a flop of 5c-8d-9c, I have bottom pair (fives) and an open-ended straight draw (needing a 6). Standard play as the Big Blind in a heads-up pot against the pre-flop raiser is to check to the aggressor. This allows me to see how they value their hand and potentially realize my equity or check-call/check-raise depending on their bet size and perceived range.

I am in position with Ace-Jack suited, holding overcard potential and backdoor flush possibilities on a connected board. While the board texture is somewhat coordinated, checking back might allow my opponent to take control on the turn. A continuation bet of 350 chips represents strength, maintains the initiative I established pre-flop, and forces the opponent to commit further with their range.

I have bottom pair (fives) and a gutshot straight draw (needing a six to complete the five-to-nine straight). Flashlight has bet 650 into a pot of 650, offering me 2:1 pot odds. My hand has significant equity: 4 outs for the straight, 3 outs to improve to two pair, and 2 outs for trips (total 9 outs). Against a Button opening range and a standard continuation bet, my bottom pair might even be ahead of some pure air bluffs. Given my massive stack advantage and the decent price, calling is the most prudent play. It allows me to realize my equity and see a turn card without over-committing to a high-variance pot while out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with 7h5h. On the turn, the board is 5c-8d-9c-2h, giving me bottom pair and an open-ended straight draw (any 6 completes the straight). The pot is 1,950 after I called Flashlight's pot-sized bet on the flop. As the out-of-position player, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they respond to the blank turn card. This allows me to control the pot size while I wait to see if I can improve on the river or reach a cheap showdown. Given my stack lead, I don't want to lead into the aggressor here and risk a raise.

The turn 2h is a blank card that does not improve my hand. Since Flash called my continuation bet on the flop, they likely have at least a pair or a strong draw, and are unlikely to fold to another bet. Checking back allows me to maintain control of the pot size, realize my equity with the flush draw, and see the river for free.

On the river, the board is 5c-8d-9c-2h-Js. I have a pair of fives. Flashlight, the pre-flop aggressor, bet pot on the flop and checked the turn. This sequence often indicates a polarized range that is either giving up on a bluff or is pot-controlling a marginal hand like a medium pair (8x or 9x) or an Ace-high. My pair of fives has some showdown value against missed draws (like clubs or 67), but it is unlikely to be the best hand if more chips go into the pot. Leading out here doesn't achieve much as I'm unlikely to get called by worse hands or fold out many better hands. Therefore, checking is the best play to realize my showdown value and see if I can win the pot for free or respond to a small bet.

I hold top pair with my Jack on the river. My opponent called a substantial flop bet but checked down the turn and river, suggesting they might have a draw that missed or a weak-to-medium hand that is now at showdown. Betting a pair of Jacks might only be called by better hands or induce a check-raise, while checking ensures I get to showdown safely to realize my equity against their potential misses.

On the button with pocket threes in a 3-handed game, this is a clear open-raise. I want to take the initiative, potentially win the blinds right away, and play a pot in position. If called, I have set-mining value and can evaluate the flop.

I am in the Small Blind with 8h 6c. The Button (Pro) has raised to 300. This is a weak hand to defend with out of position, even against a standard button open. Additionally, with Flashlight in the Big Blind, there is a risk of being squeezed. Folding is the disciplined and standard play here to preserve my healthy chip lead.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Four offsuit. The opponent has opened from the button to 300. With 200 more to call into a pot of 450, I have sufficient pot odds to defend. Ace-Four is a reasonable hand to see a flop with in position-blind matchups, and calling is a standard, low-variance play.

I am out of position in the Big Blind against the button raiser. The board is rainbow and relatively coordinated with a Queen and two Tens. My Ace-high is essentially a bluff catcher at this point, and leading into the pre-flop aggressor on this texture is ill-advised. Checking is the standard play to evaluate the opponent's continuation bet or to potentially check-fold if they show strength, as my Ace-high has very little equity against a continuing range on this board.

Checking behind to control the pot. The board is wet with broadway cards and a flush draw, hitting the Big Blind's defending range well. My pocket threes have very little equity if called, and checking allows me to realize my small equity without inflating the pot, or safely fold to future aggression.

The board is paired with Queens and Tens, and my Ace-high is very weak, functioning only as a bluff-catcher that is currently behind most of the opponent's opening range. Since the flop went check-check, both players seem to have limited interest in building a large pot. Betting here would be a pure bluff that is unlikely to force a fold from anything but total air. Checking is the most logical and conservative play to minimize potential losses and keep the pot manageable.

The turn brings a second Queen, double-pairing the board to QQTT. This completely counterfeits my pocket threes, as my hand is now QQTT with a 3 kicker, meaning virtually any two cards my opponent holds will beat me at showdown. There's no value in betting here since I would only be called by better hands, and bluffing is risky given the opponent's range likely contains many higher cards (like Ace or King high). Checking behind is the safest play to see a free river and keep the pot small.

The board currently consists of Queens full of Tens (T, T, T, Q, Q). This is the best possible 5-card hand, and because the community cards alone form this full house, every player effectively has the same hand. This results in a split pot situation where no player can improve their hand or win the pot outright. Checking is the only logical action.

The board is T-T-T-Q-Q, which is a full house (Tens full of Queens). Since I hold pocket threes, my best five-card hand is simply playing the board. The only hands that beat the board are any Ten (Quads), any Queen (Queens full of Tens), or pocket Kings/Aces (Tens full of Kings/Aces). Everything else is a chopped pot. Given the opponent defended the big blind, his range certainly contains combinations of Tens and Queens (e.g., JT, T9, QJ, KQ) which he could be slow-playing. Betting here is a 'bluff' to try and steal a chopped pot, but it risks getting called or raised by a real hand that has us crushed. Checking behind guarantees we either chop the pot or safely lose the minimum against a slow-played monster.

I am on the button with Qh3h and have a significant chip lead. This is a standard spot to open for a raise to put pressure on the blinds, especially with a suited queen. A 3x open is my standard sizing for this tournament.

I am in the small blind with a weak hand, Jack-Six offsuit. While my opponent, Flash, has an aggressive opening style on the button, J6o is not strong enough to defend effectively out of position against a raise. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop with a weak range. Given the tournament stage and my stack size, it is better to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

King-Ten suited is a strong, playable hand, especially when defending the Big Blind against a Button open. It plays well post-flop and can hit strong top pairs, straights, and flushes. Calling the raise is a standard and profitable play.

The flop comes Ace-high and paired (5c As 5d). This is a board texture that heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range, as they will have many more Aces and strong hands than I do in the Big Blind calling range. Holding King-Ten high with no direct draws, the standard and most prudent play is to check to the aggressor and likely fold to any bet.

On a dry, paired board like 5c-As-5d, the pre-flop aggressor has a significant range advantage, as I am more likely to hold an Ace than the Big Blind. This is a classic spot for a continuation bet to fold out the opponent's air and small pocket pairs. A small sizing is effective here given the board texture.

The board of 5c As 5d heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. Holding King-Ten high with only backdoor draws, I have missed the flop entirely and have very low equity against any Ace or pocket pair. Facing a continuation bet on this texture, the standard and most profitable play is to fold and preserve my stack.

Pocket Tens is a very strong hand in a 3-handed game, especially on the button. Raising to 300 is a standard move to take the initiative, build the pot, and potentially isolate or win the blinds outright.

I am in the Small Blind with 5c 2h, which is an extremely weak, unsuited, and unconnected hand. The Button has opened with a raise to 300. Calling or raising with this hand would be a statistically poor decision, so folding is the obvious and optimal move.

I am in the big blind holding Q6o. Flashlight has raised to 3x the big blind from the button. While the price to call is relatively low (200 to win 450), Q6o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position post-flop against a standard button opening range. Given my current stack lead, I prefer to wait for better spots rather than defending with marginal hands that can lead to difficult post-flop scenarios.
I have 95o on the button, which is a very weak offsuit hand. The best play is to fold and wait for a better starting hand.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Seven offsuit. The Button has folded, leaving just me and the Big Blind (Flashlight). Ten-Seven offsuit is a marginal hand, and while I only need to call 50 more to see a flop, playing it out of position against an active opponent like Flashlight is generally suboptimal. Given my significant chip lead and the tournament goal of maximizing my stack over 150 hands, I will choose to fold this weak hand and wait for more favorable situations where I have better hand equity or positional advantage.

I am on the button with pocket sixes, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. Standard aggressive play here is to raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win it pre-flop or narrow the field.

I am in the small blind holding Ten-Trey offsuit, which is a very weak hand with poor playability.
